title: Automated home-cage for the evaluation of innate non-reflexive pain behaviors in a mouse model of inflammatory pain

screening.reason: The paper reports on the use of automated home-cage monitoring to evaluate pain behaviors in mice, which is directly relevant to the review question.
matched_inclusion_criteria: Studies mice in a home-cage setting

overall_concept_summary: The study uses automated home-cage monitoring to assess pain behaviors in mice with carrageenan-induced inflammatory pain, correlating these behaviors with pharmacological interventions.
detailed_summary: This paper evaluates the use of automated home-cage monitoring for assessing non-reflexive pain behaviors in mice. It found that locomotor activity decreased and immobility increased after carrageenan injection, indicating significant pain-related behavior changes. Pharmacological intervention using indomethacin improved these behaviors.
methods_summary: Mice were monitored for locomotor activities in an automated home-cage setting following carrageenan-induced inflammatory pain. The study compared the non-reflexive pain behaviors with reflexive pain measures and pharmacological interventions.
results_summary: Automated monitoring showed reduced mobile behavior and increased immobility in mice with carrageenan-induced pain, which were correlated with pharmacological improvements.
conclusion_summary: The automated home-cage approach is useful for studying behavioral analgesia and screening analgesic drugs in mice models of inflammatory pain.
relevance_to_review: Highly relevant as it uses an automated monitoring system to assess social behavior (locomotor activity) in a mouse model of pain, which aligns with the review question.
key_takeaways: Automated home-cage monitoring can effectively capture non-reflexive pain behaviors in mice.
These behaviors are correlated with pharmacological interventions.
objective: To evaluate the use of automated home-cage monitoring for assessing innate non-reflexive pain behaviors in a mouse model of inflammatory pain.
population_or_sample: Mice induced with carrageenan-induced inflammatory pain
comparator: Reflexive pain behaviors measured via von Frey and plantar tests
methods: Automated home-cage monitoring of locomotor activities
Behavioral assessment using reflexive pain measures (von Frey, plantar tests)
outcomes_measured: Locomotor activity and immobility in mice
Correlation between non-reflexive and reflexive pain behaviors
Effect of pharmacological interventions on pain behaviors
main_results: Decreased locomotor activity and increased immobility in carrageenan-induced pain mice
Strong correlation between non-reflexive and reflexive pain behaviors
Indomethacin improved locomotor activity in pain mice
conclusions: Automated home-cage monitoring is useful for studying behavioral analgesia.
Combined evaluation of reflexive and non-reflexive pain behaviors enhances translational utility.
limitations: Limited to a single mouse model (carrageenan-induced inflammatory pain)
evidence_quotes_or_locations: Through this approach, the locomotor activities of mice with carrageenan-induced inflammatory pain can be precisely and objectively captured. Here, we found that the mobile behaviors of mice reduced, and their immobility increased, indicating that carrageenan induction in mice caused a significant decrease in locomotor activity.
